critique.summary: The article describes FilmKit, a browser-based preset manager and RAW converter for Fujifilm X-series cameras. It highlights FilmKit's features such as direct camera connection via WebUSB, preset management, RAW conversion, and live preview, while noting it is currently in beta and primarily tested on the Fujifilm X100VI.
mainPoints: FilmKit is a web application for managing Fujifilm X-series camera presets and converting RAW files using the camera's own processor.
It connects to cameras via WebUSB and supports features like preset reading, editing, writing, local preset storage, and live preview of converted images.
The app is in beta and has been tested mainly on the Fujifilm X100VI, with potential support for other X-series models that use Fujifilm's RAW conversion protocol.
FilmKit uses the Picture Transfer Protocol (PTP) over USB, similar to Fujifilm's official X RAW STUDIO software, and was developed through reverse engineering using tools like Wireshark.
Users can contribute by providing USB traffic captures for other camera models to help expand compatibility.
critique.truthfulness: The claims made in the article about FilmKit's functionality, development approach, and compatibility are consistent with information available on the official GitHub repository and technical documentation. The description of using WebUSB, PTP protocol, and reverse engineering with Wireshark aligns with known methods for interfacing with Fujifilm cameras. The app's beta status and limited testing on the X100VI are transparently stated. There are no unsupported or false claims detected.
considerations: The app's compatibility with Fujifilm X-series cameras other than the X100VI is not fully verified and may require user testing and contributions.
Users need a Chromium-based browser with WebUSB support and may need to configure system permissions (udev rules) for USB access.
As a beta project, FilmKit may have bugs or incomplete features compared to official software like Fujifilm X RAW STUDIO.
The project does not accept pull requests, so community contributions are limited to bug reports and data sharing.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around FilmKit, a webapp clone of Fujifilm's X RAW STUDIO, with users sharing their experiences and opinions on its functionality, compatibility, and the challenges of working with Fuji's proprietary raw processing. Several comments highlight the convenience of the webapp, while others note limitations with older camera models and the closed nature of Fuji's color processing algorithms.

comments.author: strogonoff
comments.text:
While file format (RAF, DNG) often is an acronym, “raw” by itself simply references raw image data; it is not an acronym, not a trademark, and does not need all caps.

The mistake of “shouting” raw is perpetuated in the wild even by serious companies, but let’s not let Apple degrade our literacy[0]. I’ll point to Adobe which does, in fact, use the correct spelling[1].

[0] It is fine when used as part of idiomatic spelling of their product or trademark (“ProRes RAW HQ”, etc.), but IIRC their promotional materials and even developer docs do shout it when simply referencing raw image data, which is a little ridiculous.

[1] https://helpx.adobe.com/camera-raw/digital-negative.html
comments.analysis: This comment provides a well-informed clarification about the term 'raw' in image processing, supported by references to Adobe's usage, making it accurate and constructive.

comments.author: deanc
comments.text:
This is pretty impressive work.

On a related note, Fuji’s simulations being locked to their walled garden has been an issue for third party tools forever. All “replications” of on device are just that. And never comparable.

I think a lot of people would like to study how they work to create true replications.
comments.analysis: The comment accurately points out the proprietary nature of Fuji's color simulations and the challenges it poses for third-party developers, reflecting a common industry understanding.

comments.author: karmasimida
comments.text:
The only reliable raw converter that can handle Fuji color is Capture One. But they have collaboration with Fuji, I don't believe that conversion algorithm is open sourced.

But it would be interesting if AI coding agent could potentially reverse engineer the algorithm.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly identifies Capture One as a leading converter for Fuji raw files due to its partnership with Fuji, noting the closed-source nature of the algorithm.

comments.author: petee
comments.text:
Great to see more Fuji X attention, their native software isn't great. Looking forward to trying it out with my older X-T20, which appears supported[1] surprisingly

I was about to mention the Fudge[2] app and its underlying library, but its already listed as a reference, nice!

[1] https://www.fujifilm-x.com/en-us/support/compatibility/softw...

[2] https://github.com/petabyt/fudge
comments.analysis: The user shares a positive outlook on FilmKit's support for older Fuji models and critiques the official software, which aligns with common user sentiment.

comments.author: enigmaticboom
comments.text:
Love this - had contemplated different setups for getting raw studio running on linux but gave up before even trying. This is exactly what I wanted - a way to play with different recipes, no install required.

It bugs out for my XT30 because the profile is a different format, but claude was able to figure out a tweak to get it running and hide some of the features the XT30 is too old for - will do the wireshark thing from a windows machine at some point.

Thank you!
comments.analysis: The comment provides a genuine user experience highlighting the convenience of the webapp, though it notes some compatibility issues with certain camera models.

comments.author: notcodingtoday
comments.text:
Wanted to edit camera profiles on Linux, couldn't get the official app to run in Wine, so I built my own https://filmkit.eggrice.soy

I also personally find the original app infuriating to use, takes a lot of click & wait to modify a profile.
comments.analysis: This comment shares a practical solution to a common problem, indicating the motivation behind FilmKit's creation and its benefits over the official app.

comments.author: wao0uuno
comments.text: This is amazing. Thank you. Seems to be working just fine with X-H2.
comments.analysis: A brief but positive user report confirming compatibility with a recent Fuji model, adding supportive anecdotal evidence.

comments.author: asah
comments.text:
Worked!! Samsung Galaxy fold 7, Chrome 146.0.7680.153, GFX 100S II firmware 1.20

MacOS 15.6.1 - could see the camera via PTP but couldn't connect (clicking "connect" didn't do anything, no error)
comments.analysis: The comment provides a mixed report of partial success and connection issues, useful for troubleshooting but limited in detail.

comments.author: p5v
comments.text: Interesting, I’ll check it out. But just like X RAW studio, I bet that it won’t work with my old X-E1.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses skepticism based on known compatibility issues with older Fuji cameras, reflecting common user experience.

comments.author: pjmlp
comments.analysis: A brief technical clarification that FilmKit is a ChromeOS platform app, adding minor but accurate context.

comments.author: zx8080
comments.text: > FilmKit uses WebUSB to connect directly to your camera, your camera's own image processor handles the conversion. FilmKit is a static client-side app, hosted on Github Pages
comments.analysis: This comment accurately describes the technical approach of FilmKit, providing useful insight into its operation.
